policy_statement: Implement the Miami-Dade 2045 Bicycle/Pedestrian Master Plan.
mechanism_description: Build and improve walking and cycling infrastructure to shift trips from cars to active transportation, reducing transport-related greenhouse gas emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Modal shift delivers the same mobility service with lower energy input per passenger-km (and often reduced total VKT), decreasing fuel/energy consumption as a direct physical consequence of using more efficient modes.
primary_causal_pathway: Travel behavior + mode choice shift (car → walk/bike/transit/micromobility) → ↓ fuel combustion / ↓ energy use in transport → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in system/human activity: Cities improve the safety, coverage, convenience, and affordability of walking, biking, transit, and shared micromobility, and/or set mode-share targets, leading travelers to substitute away from private car trips.  
2) Mediating physical process: Fewer car trips and reduced car VKT lower combustion of gasoline/diesel (and associated upstream emissions); increased use of low- or zero-direct-emission modes raises average passenger-km efficiency.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transportation-sector GHG emissions (primarily CO₂, with some reductions in CH₄ and N₂O from fuel combustion and upstream supply chains).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions by shifting trips from private cars to lower-carbon modes (walking, biking, transit, micromobility).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: shifting trips from cars to lower-carbon modes reduces fuel burned and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while co-benefits exist (air quality, safety, access), the direct and largest climate effect of the mechanism is emissions reduction from reduced car use—this is mitigation, not adaptation or nature-based action.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary:  
   - Resource Efficiency: the mobility service is provided with less energy per passenger-km via active modes and transit, inherently lowering energy demand when mode shift occurs.  
4) Edge cases applied: if a policy improves walking/biking/transit but fails to cause any shift, the intended mechanism doesn’t realize; however, the mechanism definition assumes achieved/aimed modal shift, which is fundamentally emissions-reducing. Electrification is not required for this mechanism (it’s about mode shift, not drivetrain).  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy that successfully induces modal shift toward walking/biking/transit/micromobility will, by physics/energy intensity, reduce transport fuel/energy use and thus emissions (primary mitigation), and inherently improves energy efficiency of mobility (secondary resource efficiency).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In rare contexts where added transit service is supplied by high-emitting diesel fleets and induces large rebound travel without reducing car use, net GHG benefits could be smaller; nonetheless, the canonical mechanism is defined as modal shift away from cars, which generally yields net emissions reductions.
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced heat exposure if routes include shade/cool materials; improved evacuation/last-mile mobility; public health and air quality improvements; reduced traffic fatalities—however none are specified in the source text.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be classified as infrastructure-investment (direct) if the plan implementation explicitly includes building sidewalks/protected lanes/crossings; the provided text only states plan implementation without listing capital works, so it is treated as a planning/implementation strategy (enabling).
